result:
The first light of morning always feels like a secret. It leaks in through the blinds in thin, tentative bars, as if the sun itself isn’t sure it’s welcome. I lie in bed and watch the dust motes turn into tiny planets, each one carrying some miniature civilization that will never know my name. I think about how many mornings I’ve wasted trying to fall back asleep when the universe was already busy rehearsing its next miracle. The neighbor’s cat leaps onto the windowsill, tail flicking like a metronome, and I decide that today I will not be afraid of the hours. I will let them unroll like a carpet made of every color I’ve never tasted.  

There is a kind of hunger that has nothing to do with food. It’s an itch behind the sternum, a restlessness that makes me reorganize the spice rack at 2 a.m. or alphabetize the books I haven’t read since college. I used to think it was ambition, but ambition has a destination. This is more like homesickness for a place that hasn’t been invented yet. Sometimes I feed it by riding the subway to the last stop and then walking until the street names sound fictional. I once found a playground where every swing was occupied by an adult in business attire, pumping their legs in silence, ties fluttering like prayer flags. No one looked at anyone else. I wrote a poem about it on the back of a dry-cleaning ticket, then lost the ticket in a laundromat that smelled of cardamom and regret. Maybe someone found it and thought it was a grocery list written by a very sad person: “bread, milk, forgiveness, the sound my mother made when she thought no one was listening.”  

Language keeps failing me, but I keep crawling back to it like a scandalous lover. I want words to be doors, not labels. I want to write “ocean” and feel my shoes fill with saltwater. I want to write “childhood” and suddenly be unable to reach the top shelf where my grandfather keeps the jam jars of captured fireflies. Instead, words sit on the page like well-behaved pets, wagging their tails, waiting for permission to mean something. I try tricking them: I write in the dark, I write with my non-dominant hand, I write on steamed mirrors that erase the sentence as soon as I finish it. The ghost of each letter lingers for a second, a prophecy that forgets itself.  

Yesterday I visited the museum of obsolete futures. It’s not on any map; you get there by remembering the smell of vinyl car seats on a hot day. Inside, there are dioramas of civilian space colonies that never got farther than the drawing board, plastic domes cracked by hypothetical meteor showers. A smiling mannequin family eats synthetic peas while Earth glows through the porthole like a night-light. Their dog is a robot with rubber ears that still twitch when you walk past. I asked the curator why none of the exhibits mentioned the toilets. She said excretion is never romanticized in utopias, but I suspect the real reason is that no one wants to admit how much of progress is just better ways to deal with our own waste.  

I keep a list of unfinished things: the sweater sleeve that became a snake, the apology email draft #17, the succulent I watered with green tea because someone on the internet claimed it would make the leaves shinier (it died glittering). The list grows faster than I can cross items off, a Hydra of ordinary failure. Some nights I whisper to it like a bedtime story: “Once upon a time there was a person who meant to,” and the list shivers, delighted to be inhabited by a narrative at last.  

There is comfort in the unfinished, a softness at the edges where possibility still leaks in. Completion feels like a kind of death, a door clicking shut. I think that’s why I never finish the dream where I’m teaching a classroom of bees to read. Just when the first bee sounds out the word “bloom,” I wake up. I don’t want to know whether they graduate to sonnets or simply fly away, bored.  

Memory is a prankster. It stores the smell of my elementary-school cafeteria but deletes the name of the girl who shared her pudding with me every Friday. It preserves the exact purple of the bruise I got from falling off a bike I can’t picture. Scientists say we reconstruct the past each time we recall it, like photocopying a photocopy until the original story is replaced by the story of the story. Maybe that’s why nostalgia tastes like metal: it’s the flavor of the machine rewriting us.  

I met my oldest friend for coffee last week. We used to build time machines out of refrigerator boxes and now we talk about mortgage rates. Halfway through the conversation, she pulled a marble from her pocket—one of the cat’s-eyes we hoarded in fourth grade as if they were currency in some underground economy. We rolled it back and forth across the table, watching the iris inside the glass swirl like a hurricane viewed from orbit. For a moment the café noise muted, the barista’s steam wand sounded like a tide, and we were eight again, trading futures we hadn’t yet failed to reach.  

People keep asking when I’ll settle down. The question assumes life is a suitcase that must eventually be unpacked, contents arranged in labeled drawers. But I am more like a river wearing a trench coat, trying to pass for solid. I want to keep moving until I find the place where the current forgets its name, where water holds itself so still it becomes sky. I’ve heard rumors of such a lake high in the Andes, so reflective that birds dive into their own images and emerge wet with self-recognition. I imagine camping on its shore, writing mirror-image poems that can only be read in drowning.  

The cat is now asleep on the windowsill, tail curled over her nose like a punctuation mark that refuses to end the sentence. The sun has climbed higher, turning the dust motes into gold coins that melt the moment I try to spend them. Soon the day will demand its errands, its calendars, its polite small talk about weather. But right now, in this unguarded minute, I am barefoot in the country of Almost, passport stamped with maybe. I breathe in the secret morning, exhale a flock of parentheses that drift upward, open-ended. Somewhere between the inhale and the exhale lives the entire encyclopedia of not-yet. I close my eyes, add an entry: “The word for the sound a life makes when it’s too busy becoming to notice it’s already happened.” The definition is blank, but listen— it vibrates like a bee learning the alphabet of its own wings.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
